comment_text.sn6.11:1.2: The Serpentine (sappinī) is probably the modern Panchane (from pañcānana, “five-mouthed”). There was a wanderer’s hermitage on its banks (AN 3.64, AN 4.30, AN 4.185).
comment_text.sn6.11:1.3: Sanaṅkumāra (“Everyoung”) became a Hindu deity closely associated with the worship of Krishna. He first appears in the seventh chapter of the Chāndogya Upaniṣad. He speaks this verse also at DN 3:1.28.2, DN 27:32.2, MN 53:25.3, AN 11.10:6.1, and it was endorsed by the Buddha. He features in two extended discussions among the gods, his appearance heralded by a great light in the north (DN 18:15.1, DN 19:15.1). He also appears in DN 20:20.3.

root_text.sn6.11:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati sappinītīre.
root_text.sn6.11:1.3: Atha kho brahmā sanaṅkumāro abhikkantāya rattiyā abhikkantavaṇṇo kevalakappaṁ sappinītīraṁ obhāsetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn6.11:1.4: Ekamantaṁ ṭhito kho brahmā sanaṅkumāro bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn6.11:3.3: Atha kho brahmā sanaṅkumāro “samanuñño me satthā”ti bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn6.11:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, on the bank of the Serpentine river.
translation_text.sn6.11:1.3: Then, late at night, the glorious divinity Sanaṅkumāra, lighting up the entire Serpentine riverbank, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side,
translation_text.sn6.11:1.4: and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn6.11:2.2: who take clan as the standard.
translation_text.sn6.11:2.3: But one accomplished in knowledge and conduct
translation_text.sn6.11:2.4: is best among gods and humans.”
translation_text.sn6.11:3.1: That’s what the divinity Sanaṅkumāra said,
translation_text.sn6.11:3.3: Then the divinity Sanaṅkumāra, knowing that the teacher approved, bowed and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before vanishing right there.
